text: Welcome to another video of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about basics of transabdominal preperitoneal ventral hernia repair. Many of us consider this a gateway into robotic abdominal wall reconstruction. And so we'll review a case of a forty five year old man. His driven inflammatory bowel disease. He had a multi recurrent ventral incisional hernia at the epigastrum. This was previously a site for an extraction of a small bowel after resection laparoscopically. A few other abdominal procedures and a primary repair in the past, epigastric hernia.

The surgeon performs a transabdominal preperitoneal mesh repair, beginning with an incision into the peritoneum. The dissection progresses cephalad beyond the midline and caudally towards the hernia, revealing the volcano sign as the hernia sac is reduced and separated from the pseudosac. The contralateral preperitoneal dissection is then developed.

The surgeon carefully dissects the transversalis fascia, identifying its two leaflets to efficiently develop the space. Electrocautery is used to separate tissue without creating peritoneal holes, resulting in a broad space prepared for the placement of a 15x15 polypropylene mesh.

The surgeon uses a permanent V-lock suture to close the hernia defect, starting a few centimeters caudal and moving cephalad. The defect is closed in a vertical orientation, measuring approximately 2.5 centimeters wide and 5 centimeters long. Sequential tightening is performed under insufflation pressure between 8 and 12 mmHg.

The surgeon re-approximates the tissue edges and runs the suture back, taking stitches between previous bites to avoid overplicating the tissue. The V-lock suture is locked, providing broad coverage over the closed hernia defect. The peritoneal flap is developed to accommodate the fifteen by fifteen polypropylene mesh.

The surgeon assesses the mesh placement on the anterior abdominal wall, opting for two fixation points due to its stable position. As the abdomen is desufflated, the mesh achieves good apposition with the tissue. The peritoneal flap is closed using a three-oh V-lock suture on a V-twenty needle, running left to right on the screen. A small hole is visible around the prior hernia defect.

And we just pull out that polysorb or absorbable braided suture that we had used for mesh fixation. And that's the case. Forty-five minutes total operative time. We do about four to seven of these in a day. The patient was discharged the same day. Again, I think a great use case for robotics. Thank you very much.
